coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and multifunctional application of two novel carbon quantum dots as corrosion inhibitors

coredata.dc:description: N, S- and N-doped carbon quantum dots (CDs) were synthesized by calcination in the presence of L-cysteine and 2-aminobutyric acid by using P-phenylenediamine as the raw material. The CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Results show that both kinds of CDs have spherical structures and particle sizes. The CDs were dispersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and doped into the epoxy coating to prepare the composite coating. The two kinds of CDs have good corrosion inhibition effects in both applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Corrosion inhibitors
Carbon steel
Composite coatings

Characterization of N-CDs and N, S-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis method of N-CDs and N, S-CDs; Color of ethanol solutions of N-CDs and N, S-CDs under fluorescent lamp (left) and ultraviolet lamp (right).
(a)TEM of N-CDs, (b)TEM of N, S-CDs, (c) FTIR of synthetic materials and two carbon quantum dots, (d) XRD of N-CDs and N, S-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs and N, S-CDs. (b/d) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N, S-CDs, and N-CDs. (c/e) FL emission spectra N, S-CDs, and N-CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
High-resolution XPS spectra of N-CDs and N, S-CDs (a) full spectra, (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) O 2p, (e) S 2p.
The SEM images of coupons surface after immersion in different solution (a)Blank, (b)30 ppm N-CDs, (c)30 ppm N, S-CDs. (a1/b1/c1) Corresponding EDS data.
Contact angle measurements on the surface of carbon steel after immersion in (a) blank, (b) 30 ppm N-CDs, (c) 30 ppm N, S-CDs. (d/e/f) AFM results for steel surfaces under different conditions.
XPS spectra of steel surface, (a) XPS survey spectra, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Fe 2p, (c) S 2p, (d) N 1 s.
The open circuit potential of carbon steel varies with soaking time in solutions containing N, S-CDs and N-CDs at different concentrations.
EIS images of steel immersion in various test solutions (a) (c) (e) N, S-CDs; (b) (d) (f) N-CDs.
Equivalent circuits used to fit the impedance spectra for carbon steel immersion in different solutions.
Potentiodynamic polarization curves of N80 steel in various test solutions (a) N, S-CDs; (b) N-CDs.
Corrosion inhibition mechanism of carbon quantum dots in solution.

Nyquist and Bode diagrams of carbon steel immersion in different solutions (a), (c), (e) (g), (i), (k) and (m) is the Nyquist diagram of the pure epoxy coating, 0.5 wt% N-CDs, 0.5 wt% N, S-CDs, 1 wt% N-CDs,1 wt% N, S-CDs, 2 wt%N-CDs, 2 wt% N, S-CDs epoxies respectively; (b)、(d)、(f)、(h)、(j)、(l) and (n)is the Bode diagram of the pure epoxy coating, 0.5 wt% N-CDs, 0.5 wt% N, S-CDs, 1 wt% N-CDs,1 wt% N, S-CDs, 2 wt% N-CDs, 2 wt% N, S-CDs epoxies respectively.
Corrosion inhibition mechanism of carbon quantum dots in coatings.
Elemental analysis result of N-CDs and N,S-CDs.
Impedance parameters of carbon steel immersion in different solutions.
Potentiodynamic polarization parameters of carbon steel immersion in different solutions.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and multifunctional application of two novel carbon quantum dots as corrosion inhibitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Material Chemistry of Energy Conversion and Storage, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Material Chemistry of Energy Conversion and Storage, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Material Chemistry of Energy Conversion and Storage, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology Wuhan 430074, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xi’an Changqing Chemical Group Co. Ltd, Xi’an 710018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xi’an Changqing Chemical Group Co. Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xi’an Changqing Chemical Group Co. Ltd, Xi’an 710018, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N, S- and N-doped carbon quantum dots (CDs) were synthesized by calcination in the presence of L-cysteine and 2-aminobutyric acid by using P-phenylenediamine as the raw material. The CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Results show that both kinds of CDs have spherical structures and particle sizes. The CDs were dispersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and doped into the epoxy coating to prepare the composite coating. The two kinds of CDs have good corrosion inhibition effects in both applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel is widely utilized in aviation, shipping industry, building steel, and oil field gas transmission because of its great mechanical qualities, excellent thermal conductivity, and low-cost performance [1,2]. However, carbon steel is particularly prone to corrosion, especially in an environment containing chloride ions, resulting in a reduced service life. It also causes huge economic losses and serious environmental damage. So, taking anticorrosion measures is necessary [3]. The most typical measures are applying corrosion inhibitors and brushing modified coatings on metal surfaces [4].

However, most of the traditional corrosion inhibitors pollute the environment, which is contrary to the concept of “green hills and clear water;” coating is prone to stress corrosion cracking, and corrosion inhibitors are often added [5]. The existing problems could be solved when a green corrosion inhibitor with good corrosion resistance is developed and doped into the coating [6].

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are amorphous nanocarbon materials that are non-toxic and environmentally benign, with strong water solubility and biocompatibility [7]. CDs have been widely used in bioimaging, photocatalysis, metal corrosion prevention, and other sectors owing to their unique properties in recent years. Cao et al. [8] prepared N-doped CDs (N-CDs) and discovered that increasing the corrosion inhibitor concentration can improve the corrosion resistance of CDs, with the highest corrosion resistance efficiency of 97.8 % at 30 ppm. The slope of the Tafel curve for the cathode and anode and the rate of metal corrosion steadily decreased as the inhibitor concentration was increased. The potentiodynamic polarization curve suggested that N-CDs may completely stop carbon steel from corrosion in an acidic solution. On the basis of the idea that CDs can adsorb on the surface of a metal substrate and create a barrier film, Cui et al. [9] proved that low-cytotoxicity CDs are environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors for metal products and showed a direct relationship between the amount of CDs present in the electrolyte and their ability to prevent corrosion. CDs were created from tea by Konwar et al. [10], who also investigated how they affected the characteristics of chitosan hydrogel film. The combination of chitosan and CDs produced nanofilms with remarkable thermal stability and a barrier to ultraviolet (UV) and visible light. The CDs increased the chitosan films’ water contact angle. The density of hydrophobic aromatic groups on the membrane surface was increased by the presence of CDs. However, only a few research in the field of coatings has documented CDS use in polymer coatings. For example, a self-healing corrosion-resistant coating was prepared by Zhu et al. [11] by incorporating CDs into a polymer matrix. The results demonstrated an improvement in the polymer coating’s self-healing and anticorrosion abilities. The interaction between CDs and polymer led to an improvement in the performance of polymer/CDs nanocomposite coating. Ramezanzadeh et al. [12] prepared graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) with a well-crystalline single-atom layered structure, in the size ranging from 2 nm to 20 nm, by using a bottom-up approach to directly pyrolyze citric acid, followed by a quick mixing polymerization step. Then, polyaniline (PANI) was used for functionalization. Finally, the effect of GOQD-PANI on the corrosion resistance of the epoxy coating was examined using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and salt spray test. The results showed that adding GOQD-PAI significantly enhanced the barrier and inhibitory properties of epoxy coatings. Fine GOQD-PANI particles were used to fill the epoxy resin matrix, blocking any gaps or faults that may have existed. Ye Y et al. [13] utilized functional CDs produced from the derivatives of citric acid as intercalators to modify graphene and then disseminated the CDs throughout an epoxy resin matrix to form a coating known as CDs-modified graphene/epoxy resin. Its microstructure, self-healing capabilities, and corrosion resistance were examined at the same time. According to structural characterization, the functionalized CDs and graphene interacted to produce the “Π” effect. The CDs greatly enhanced the interfacial compatibility and dispersion of graphene. Electrochemical tests showed that the EP coating, which was made by combining 0.5 wt% CDs-G with epoxy resin, has exceptional steel-protecting properties.

Inspired by the above research, N-CDs and N, S-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) were synthesized by burning at high temperatures. P-phenylenediamine (PPD) was used as the carbon source, and L-cysteine and 2-aminobutyric acid were used as the dopants. The corrosion inhibition effects of the two CDs on carbon steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution was compared using electrochemical testing. The CDs were then incorporated into coatings to further explore their use.

PPD (99 %), L-cysteine (99 %), 2-aminobutyric acid, NaOH (analytical reagent), and NaCl (analytical reagent) were purchased from Aladdin Holdings Group. Ethanol was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Distilled water was made in the laboratory.

For the experiment, Shandong Senke Co., Ltd. supplied the carbon steel, which has the following chemical composition: (wt%) 0.34 % C, 0.29 % Si, 1.39 % Mn, 0.015 % P, 0.013 % S, 0.45 % Cr, 0.028 % Ni, and 0.3 % Mo. The carbon steel was balanced with Fe, and before it was used, the surface was cleaned with an ultrasonic cleaner and ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs and N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis method of N, S-CDs was based on the method of Bao et al. [14], with some modifications. PPD (0.6 g) and L-cysteine (1.2 g) were mixed well, and then 1.5 mL of 1 M NaOH was added and ground fully. The mixture was transferred to a crucible and reacted at 250 °C for 3 h. Subsequently, the crude product was dissolved and filtered, and the yellow solution obtained was filtered for 36 h by using a 1000 D dialysis bag to remove the unreacted precursors. Finally, pure solid N, S-CDs powder was obtained by freeze drying for 48 h. N-CDs were synthesized similarly, except that L-cysteine was replaced with 2-aminobutyric acid.

TEM (Field Emission Instruments, USA) was used to measure the size and morphology of the samples. The XRD patterns were characterized using X’Pert PRO (PANalytical B.V., Netherlands). Fluorescence spectrophotometry (JASCO, FP-6500, Japan) was used to characterize the fluorescence spectrum. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Bruker Instrument, Germany), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS SUPRA+, Japan), and UV–Vis spectroscopy (Shimadzu, Japan) were used to analyze the chemical structure and composition of CDs.

Three steel sheets were cleaned before being submerged for 12 h in a solution containing 30 ppm of N, S-CDS and N-CDs. After being cleaned with acetone and distilled water, the steel sheets were chilled-air dried before being placed in a vacuum-drying oven. SEM (Phillips Quanta 200 SEM system) was used to examine the corrosion morphology of the sample surface, and EDS and XPS (AXIS-ULTRA DLD-600 W with Al K excitation source) were used to examine the surface composition of the samples.

The test samples were polished with a diamond polishing paste to reduce the surface roughness and then placed in different solutions for 12 h before tests were performed. The contact angle of each test piece was tested three times by using a contact angle tester (JC2000, Powereach), and the average value was obtained. AFM (SPM-9700, Shimadzu, Japan) under tapping mode was used to observe the samples.

CDs powder (0.08 g) was ultrasonically dissolved in 5 mL acetone and then mixed with 6 g of epoxy resin. The mixture was placed in a drying oven set at 60 °C after 30 min of mechanical stirring to help the acetone in the mixture evaporate. Then, 2 g of water-based curing agent was added to the mixture. The mixture was mechanically stirred for 30 min and vacuumed to remove small bubbles. Finally, the composite coating was obtained.
For comparison, a blank coating was prepared using the same method but without adding CDs powder.

Electrochemical tests were performed on a carbon steel in 3.5 wt% NaCl electrolyte by using an electrochemical workstation (CHI-660E) at room temperature, with the CDs dissolved in the electrolyte at different concentrations for each test. A standard three-electrode system with a working electrode contact surface of 1 cm2 made of carbon steel, a platinum electrode, and a reference saturated calomel electrode (Hg-HgCl2) was used in this experiment. The test solution was newly configured before the experiment to guarantee the repeatability of the experimental data. Firstly, the open circuit potential was monitored for 12 h. Then before EIS testing, the open circuit potential (1800 s) was measured to make the system reach a dynamic equilibrium. The frequency and amplitude of EIS were 105–10−2 Hz and 5 mV, respectively. For potentiodynamic polarization measurements, the scanning range corresponded from −150 mV to +150 mV relative to the open circuit potential, along with 0.5 mV/s scanning rate. Both tests under these conditions take about 30 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-CDs and N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The two types of CDs were evenly spread in ethanol, and they appeared brown under natural light. However, when exposed to UV radiation at 365 nm, they glowed brightly. Fig. 1 shows the manufacturing processes of the two CDs, and Table 1 clearly shows their elemental composition. N, S-CDs had 6.8 % S element concentration.

The shape and structure of N-CDS and N, S-CDs were examined using TEM. The two types of CDs exhibited amorphous spherical formations without evident aggregation, as seen in Fig. 2 [15]. The size of the latter was significantly larger than that of the former, may be because N, S-CDs are doped more than N-CDs [16].

FTIR spectroscopy was used to compare and analyze the two kinds of CDs and the synthetic raw materials to determine the change in the material structure in the reaction process. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the band at 749 cm−1 was generated by the C-H of the benzene ring due to out-of-plane bending vibration. The IR spectra of L-cysteine showed the stretching vibrations of –NH2, –SH, and CO at 3181, 2060, and 1578 cm−1, respectively [17]. Meanwhile, the stretching vibrations of –NH2, CO, and C–H were represented by the infrared wavelengths of 2-aminobutyric acid at 3360, 1635, and 1517 cm−1, respectively [18]. The stretching vibrations of the –NH2, CC, and benzene rings were represented by the infrared absorption peaks of PPD at 3000, 2130, and 1585 cm−1, respectively [19]. The diagram shows that the different absorption peaks of the two carbon sites are distinct from those of their predecessors, and that their locations are essentially the same. The stretching vibrations of –NH2/–OH and the benzene ring caused peaks to appear at 3443 and 1657 cm−1, respectively. Additionally, a weak peak for N, S-CDs at 2060 cm−1 was found, brought on by the stretching vibration of –SH [20]. The aforementioned findings provide preliminary evidence that CDs were successfully doped with N and S elements.

Fig. 2(d) displays the XRD patterns of the two types of CDs. N-CDs and N, S-CDs had broad diffraction peaks at 2Θ of 23.1° and 20.9°, respectively, with 0.39 and 0.43 nm layer spacings, respectively. Both layers were much further spaced apart than graphene (0.34 nm), proving that adding heteroatoms to CDs can increase the layer spacing of amorphous carbon compounds [21,22]. The layer spacing of N, S-CDs was larger than that of N-CDs because more heteroatoms were introduced.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDS and N, S-CDs were characterized to analyze their optical characteristics. As shown in Fig. 3(a), a slight difference was observed in the positions of the characteristic absorption peaks of the two CDs. These peaks were around 210 and 280 nm, and they belong to the Π-Π* transition of the aromatic hydrocarbon sp2 structure and the n-Π* transition of CO, respectively [23]. Fig. 3(b) and 3(d) depict the fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs and N-CDs. The results revealed that the maximum emission wavelength of N, S-CDs was 534 nm, which is related to their excitation wavelength of 433 nm. Meanwhile, the excitation wavelength of N-CDs was 370 nm, and their maximum emission wavelength was 460 nm. Fig. 3(c) and 3(e) show the emission spectra of the two CDs at different excitation wavelengths. The results demonstrated the correlation between the emission spectra of the two CDs and the excitation signal, which is a characteristic of CDs fluorescence emission. The absorption peak in the fluorescence emission spectrum similarly redshifted as the excitation wavelength increased [24,25].

Additionally, the elemental composition and bonding patterns were examined using XPS. Fig. 4(a) displays the profiles of N-CDs and N, S-CDs obtained from the XPS complete scan, showing the successful doping of N and S elements. The bonding states of various atoms were then ascertained by analyzing the XPS profiles of various elements at the two carbon sites. As shown in Fig. 4(b), three peaks at 284.6, 285.8, and 288.3 eV, which corresponded to CC/C–C, C–O/C–N/C-S, and CO/CS, respectively, can be produced by fitting the C 1S spectrum [26,27]. The covalent bonds between pyridinium and pyrrole were represented by the spectra of N 1 s [Fig. 4(c)], which may be separated into two peaks at 399.2 and 400.1 eV, respectively. Two peaks at 530.8 and 532.8 eV were observed in the spectra of O 1 s in Fig. 4(d), corresponding to CO and C–O, respectively [28,29]. Moreover, two peaks at 164.1 and 168.9 eV, which corresponded to CS/ C–S and –C–S(O)X–C, respectively, can be seen in the fit of S 2p in Fig. 4(e) [30,31]. The S 2p spectrum revealed new S-containing bonds in N,S-CDs, consistent with the results of UV–vis and FTIR, demonstrating that the precursor successfully synthesized two doped CDs by high-temperature calcination. The N 1 s spectrum revealed that both CDs contained N hetero-ring structures [32].

The SEM characterization of the samples after immersion is shown in Fig. 5. Fig. 5(a) shows that the samples soaked in a blank solution had severe surface corrosion, corrosion pits, and corrosion products of different sizes. Fig. 5(b) shows that the sample soaked in N-CDs-containing solution had some cracks on the surface and not too serious corrosion. Fig. 5(c) shows the sample soaked in N, S-CDs-containing solution. The carbon steel surface, which is comparatively smooth in comparison to the previous two images, had nearly no cracks. In addition, comparison of the corresponding EDS images of the three [Fig. 5(a1), 5(b1), and 5(c1)] shows that the Fe content on the metal surface decreased after adding a corrosion inhibitor. This finding indicated an increase in the rate at which corrosion inhibitor molecules cover the surface of carbon steel. Additionally, the surface of carbon steel displayed N elements after being soaked in N-CDs-containing solution, whereas N and S elements were found after soaking in N, S-CDs-containing solution. This finding showed that the molecules of the corrosion inhibitors bound to the surface of the carbon steel and established covalent bonds with Fe.

Three samples were tested using contact angles to determine how hydrophobic their surfaces are [33]. Fig. 6 depicts the sample’s contact angle with water after soaking in various scenarios. The sample soaked in blank solution had the least contact angle, followed by the samples soaked in N-CDs- and N, S-CDs-containing solutions. This finding showed that the corrosion inhibitor film formed by N, S-CDs on the surface of carbon steel has the strongest hydrophobicity [34]. The hydrophobic film that the CDs corrosion inhibitor forms on the surface of the material can isolate metal from corrosive media and prevent metal corrosion.

AFM images in Fig. 6 depict the surface roughness of carbon steel in three states [35,36]. A hump-like irregular surface was visible on the surface of carbon steel immersed in blank solution, suggesting that the metal severely corroded. The surface roughness value was 35.850 nm. Contrarily, the surface immersed in a solution containing N, S-CDs corrosion inhibitor was the smoothest, with a surface roughness value of 7.238 nm, followed by that immersed in a solution containing N-CDs corrosion inhibitor, with a surface roughness value of 19.407 nm. Both kinds of CDs corrosion inhibitors effectively inhibited the corrosion of carbon steel, with the N, S-CDs corrosion inhibitor demonstrating a better corrosion inhibition effect.

The elemental composition and binding state of carbon steel soaked in different solutions were studied by XPS. Taking carbon steel in blank solution as the control group, as shown in Fig. 7(a) of the full spectrum, it can be seen that two kinds of carbon quantum dots were indeed adsorbated on the surface of carbon steel. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the peaks at 710.2 eV and 712.3 eV correspond to Fe2O3 and FeOOH respectively. It can be seen that the content of FeOOH on the surface of carbon steel soaked in carbon quantum dots solution is significantly reduced, which may be because the addition of carbon quantum dots inhibits the cathode reaction process. In addition, Fig. 7(c) and 6 (d) proved that the N and S elements in the two carbon quantum dots form Fe-N and Fe-S with the iron surface. It is proved that two kinds of quantum dots were adsorbed on metal surface by physical adsorption and chemical adsorption at the same time.

The change of open circuit potential (OCP) with electrode soaking time is an important basis for exploring the action behavior of corrosion inhibitors. As shown in Fig. 8, when N,S-CDs of different concentrations were added, the corrosion potential moved negatively. When different concentrations of N-CDs were added, the corrosion potential moved positively. The movement of corrosion potential is positively correlated with the concentration of corrosion inhibitor, and the corrosion potential is gradually stable with the extension of soaking time.

Combined with the above surface analysis, it is further clear that the two corrosion inhibitors can successfully adsorb on the surface of carbon steel to form a protective film and inhibit metal corrosion.

Fig. 9 shows the EIS of carbon steel in different solutions. The Nyquist diagram has characteristic capacitive arcs, as seen in Fig. 7(a) and 7(b), indicating that charge transfer is the primary factor regulating the corrosion of carbon steel. The width and height of the impedance arc grew in comparison to that under the blank solution as the concentration of the corrosion inhibitor increased, but the impedance arc corresponding to N, S-CDs was more noticeably altered at the same concentration. This finding demonstrated how the two corrosion inhibitors work together to create a shield-like film that protects the metal and prevents corrosion. The N, S-CDs corrosion inhibitor has better corrosion inhibition efficacy [37,38].

Two curves representing impedance modulus and phase angle in the frequency range of 105–10−2 are shown in the Bode diagram. The Bode diagrams indicate that the charge transfer process may primarily regulates the corrosion of carbon steel in NaCl solution [39,40].

The equivalent circuit was used to fit the impedance arc, as shown in Fig. 10, where Rs is the resistance of the solution between the reference electrode and the metal/working electrode, Rct denotes the charge transfer resistance of carbon steel and can be utilized to assess the corrosion level, Qdl is the constant phase element related to the double-layer capacitance, and Qf represents the capacitance of the film. The related electrochemical parameters obtained from the EIS measurements are listed in Table 2.

The value of Rs for the two corrosion inhibitors slightly changed only under various circumstances, demonstrating that the characteristics of the solution are unaffected by the addition of corrosion inhibitors [41]. The Rct and Rf values increased with the increase in corrosion inhibitor concentration. They were higher when N, S-CDs were added at the same concentration as N-CDs. This finding showed that both corrosion inhibitors effectively form a protective film on the metal surface, with N, S-CDs having a better corrosion inhibition effect.

The potentiodynamic polarization curves of carbon steel in different solutions are shown in Fig. 11. Table 3 displays the polarization curves’ fitted parameters such as the corrosion potential (Ecorr), corrosion potential density (Icorr), and anode and cathode Tafel slopes (ba, bc). The following formula was used to determine the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) at an appropriate concentration:
(1)
η ( % )
=
icorr , 0 − icorr
icorr , 0
× 100 % ,
where icorr is the current density in the presence of corrosion inhibitors, and icorr,0 is the current density in the absence of corrosion inhibitors.

As shown in Fig. 11(b), with the increase in N-CDs inhibitor concentration, the icorr decreased, the corrosion inhibition efficiency increased, and the corrosion potential shifted significantly positively, indicating that the anode electrochemical reaction was significantly inhibited. As shown in Fig. 11(a), the corrosion current density evidently changed when N, S-CDs were added under the same concentration, indicating that N, S-CDs have a better corrosion inhibition effect. However, the corrosion potential shifted significantly negatively when N, S-CDs were added, indicating that the cathodic corrosion inhibition of the inhibitor is dominant. The change in corrosion potential was less than 85 mV for both types of corrosion inhibitors, showing that they are both mixed-type corrosion inhibitors [42], with N, S-CDs being the dominant cathode corrosion inhibitor [43]. The corrosion inhibition efficiencies of N-CDs and N, S-CDs at 30 ppm were 64 % and 71 %, respectively. This finding showed that the corrosion inhibition effect of N, S-CDs is superior than that of N-CDS, because the latter has one more heteroatom “S” than the former, resulting in more adsorption sites on the metal substrate, which provides a better corrosion inhibition effect.

Fig. 12 illustrates how CDs in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution can prevent the corrosion of carbon steel. The electrochemical and surface analysis results suggested that CDs create a shield on the metal’s surface, preventing contact between the metal and the corrosive liquid and acting as an anticorrosion agent. According to the results of elemental analysis, heteroatoms were present in the quantum dots. Heteroatoms may easily fill the vacant 3d orbitals of Fe atoms because they have lone pairs of electrons, creating coordination bonds and adhering to the metal surface. The remaining CDs diffused to the metal surface by the agglomeration effect, and a portion of the CDs in the solution adsorbed on the metal surface, thereby protecting the metal.

EIS was used to study the anticorrosive properties of different coatings in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The electrochemical measurement results of the coatings under different soaking times are shown by the Nyquist and Bode diagrams in Fig. 13.

The three steps of the corrosion process are often separated. If micro-holes, fissures, or other coating flaws are present, the corrosive medium could start to penetrate the coating faster in the initial stage. Since the EIS image only contains one time constant at this stage, the coating is analogous to a barrier layer with a high resistance and low capacitance. As the immersion process continues, the coating resistance decreases and the capacitance increases. However, the metal substrate has not yet been exposed to the corrosive medium. In the second step, the metal substrate is exposed to the corrosive fluid, at which stage the corrosion reaction occurs, the metal substrate displays two-time constant characteristics in the EIS image, and the coating slowly degrades and fails.

In this experiment, different coatings were prepared: blank (A), coatings coated with 0.5 wt% (B1), 1 wt% (B2), and 2 wt% (B3) N-CDs; and coatings coated with 0.5 wt% (C1), 1 wt% (C2), and 2 wt% (C3) N, S-CDs.

As shown in Fig. 13, the impedance modulus of C2 coating was the highest, and the value of |Z|f=0.01HZ was 8.54 × 109 ohm·cm2. One time constant only appeared during the whole soaking process, and the value of impedance modulus did not significantly decrease, indicating that the coating has the best anticorrosion effect when doped with 1 wt% N, S-CDs. The anti-corrosive effect of the prepared coating was ranked as follows: C2 > B2 > C3 > B3 > C1 > B1 > A.

For the blank coating without corrosion inhibitor added, the value of |Z|f=0.01HZ decreased from 1.01 × 106 ohm·cm2 to 4.81 × 105 ohm·cm2 after 1D immersion, at which time the value of |Z| was already lower than 106 ohm·cm2, and two-time constants and diffusion appeared in the EIS diagram. In general, a coating is thought to have lost its protective efficacy on a metal substrate when its low-frequency impedance falls below 106 ohm·cm2. The coating’s low-frequency impedance modulus decreased to 5.71 × 104 ohm·cm2 at 11D with the increase in soaking time, showing that the coating underwent substantial degradation and that the corrosive medium diffused within it.

The manufactured CDs corrosion inhibitor can remarkably boost the coating’s corrosion resistance, because the impedance modulus values of the coatings with different numbers of N-CDs and N, S-CDs were higher by 2 or 3 orders of magnitude than those of the blank coating. Two-time constants for the B1 coating and the C1 coating appeared at D5 and D20, respectively, and no diffusion was observed, proving that the metal substrate was affected by the corrosion.

The mechanism diagram is shown in Fig. 14 to further understand the anticorrosion effect of CDs in the coating. For blank coatings, flaws like voids and micropores are created due to solvent volatilization, which allows corrosive media to permeate the metal substrate and corrode the metal. Due to the abundance of hydroxyl, amino, and amide groups on the surface of CDs, the addition of CDs can successfully limit the penetration of corrosive media to provide the anti-corrosion effect. A covalent link between the amide group and epoxy resin can help strengthen the bonding power between CDs and epoxy resin. Hydroxyl and amino groups can create covalent and hydrogen bonds with one another to connect the CDs and act as a protective film for the metal substrate, thus increasing the coating’s ability to resist corrosion.

In this work, N- and N, S-CDs were synthesized using PPD, L-cysteine, and 2-aminobutyric acid as precursors. CDs were successfully established, as demonstrated using infrared, XRD, TEM, and fluorescence techniques. The electrochemical experiments demonstrated that the two different types of CDs have effective anticorrosion properties in solutions and coatings. The synthesis method is simple and efficient, and it provides a new idea for the synthesis of multifunctional anticorrosion materials.
Zhenyu Chen: Supervision. Yubo Lian: Funding acquisition. Chen Huang: Writing – original draft. Qishuang Yang: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51871105) for their financial support and the analysis support of the Analytical and Testing Center, Huazhong University of Science and Technology.
